title: God's Sovereignty and Alexander the Great
speaker: Dr. John Battle
source_files: God's Sovereignty and Alexander the Great - Dr. John Battle.txt

outline.point: I. Introduction: God Uses History for His Purposes
subpoints: A. Unsettling world events and the comfort of divine sovereignty
B. Alexander the Great as a case study in providential history
C. Three themes: fulfilled prophecy, conquest, Greek New Testament

outline.point: II. Alexander Fulfills Old Testament Prophecy
subpoints: A. Ezekiel 26: the scraping of Tyre literally fulfilled by Alexander
B. Daniel 2: the bronze section of the statue represents Greece
C. Daniel 7: the four-winged leopard with four heads
D. Daniel 8: the goat with a prominent horn destroys the Persian ram
E. Daniel 10-11: angelic warfare involving the Prince of Greece

outline.point: III. How God Gave Alexander His Empire
subpoints: A. The Greek city-states united against Persia, then under Macedonia
B. Philip of Macedon and Alexander rising to power at age 20
C. Three decisive battles against Persia (Granicus, Issus, Gaugamela)
D. The march through Palestine, Egypt, and to the Indus River

outline.point: IV. Alexander Prepares the Way for the Gospel
subpoints: A. Greek became the universal language of culture and government
B. Hellenistic Greek enabled the writing of the New Testament
C. Paul traveled the same routes Alexander forged (Troy to Macedonia)
D. God used a conqueror to spread the language that would carry the gospel

summary:
In this Sunday School lecture, Dr. John Battle of Western Reformed Seminary demonstrates that Alexander the Great's rapid conquests (ca. 336-323 BC) were precise fulfillments of prophecies written over 200 years earlier in Ezekiel and Daniel. From the literal scraping of Tyre to the four-horned division of his empire, every major prophetic detail was fulfilled exactly, confirming that God governs the rise and fall of nations for his own purposes.

Beyond fulfilled prophecy, Dr. Battle shows how God used Alexander to prepare the ancient world for the gospel. By spreading Hellenistic Greek throughout the Middle East, North Africa, and Central Asia, Alexander unknowingly created the linguistic infrastructure through which the New Testament would be written and the apostles would preach. The most unknown Christian is more precious in God's sight than the greatest conqueror—yet God uses even conquerors to build his church.
transcript:
[Note: This lecture was introduced by Pastor Tito Lyro, who introduced Dr. Battle as Professor of Theology and New Testament at Western Reformed Seminary, a founding president and board member of the Seminary.]

I brought a couple of things to show you so you can see what I'm talking about — and I also wrote this so I can see what I'm talking about. Today I thought it would be fun to look at the way that God uses historical events for his purposes.

As you know, there's a lot going on in the world right now. Many of you have been following the terrible events in South Africa — the tremendous dangers there, the various leaders that have come and gone, a former president inspiring riots and seeking to impose a communist dictatorship on that country. You need to remember the people of God there in prayer. But a lot of times you see these things and think, things are out of hand. It's comforting to know that when you look back in history, you see things equally upsetting and terrible — death and mayhem for thousands of people, great leaders risen up and cut down. And God has a purpose in all of it. He is furthering his own cause and helping his own people through the course of history.

I thought it would be fun to look at one example: a very famous historical figure — conqueror, dictator, he goes by many titles. He's been admired by many and cursed by many, but he had a tremendous impact on the world. As far as we're concerned, Alexander the Great shows us the power of God to control the events of history for his purposes. I'd like for us to see how Alexander fulfilled the prophecies God had already placed in his Word, and how he prepared the way for the Lord Jesus Christ and his apostles to spread the gospel throughout the world. Alexander had no idea about those things, but as we look back, we see that was his main significance. So we're going to look at three things: how he fulfilled prophecy, how he conquered a great empire, and how he prepared the way for the Greek New Testament.

## I. Alexander Fulfills Old Testament Prophecy

Alexander lived a little before 300 BC. I'll say BC rather than BCE — "before Christ" has worked pretty well for over a thousand years. Many years before Alexander was born, prophecies were written in the Old Testament that are about him specifically. These occur in Ezekiel and in Daniel, written about 200 years before Alexander came.

### Ezekiel 26: The Prophecy Against Tyre

Ezekiel 26 says, "This is what the sovereign Lord says: I am against you, O Tyre. I will bring many nations against you like the sea casting up its waves. They will destroy the walls of Tyre and pull down her towers. I will scrape away her rubble and make her a bare rock out in the sea. She will become a place to spread fishnets."

Now what is Tyre? It's a city in Lebanon, on the coast just north of Israel — a very famous city throughout history. And the interesting thing is that this is exactly what Alexander the Great did to the city of Tyre.

Tyre was difficult to conquer because it was in two sections. There was the city right on the coast, and then half a mile away was an island in the Mediterranean. Whenever Tyre came under attack, the people would simply retreat to the island with their boats. The army on land could not attack them, and the people on the island could send ships out for supplies and reinforcements. The city seemed impregnable.

When Alexander came, the population had all escaped to the island, half a mile from the coast. Alexander's strategy was simply — a very brutal strategy — to clear away all the rocks, all the rubble, everything, and dump it into the sea. It wasn't very deep. They actually made a causeway from the mainland out to the island. He started using soldiers, then used captive peoples as slaves, and gradually they worked toward the island. When they completed that causeway, the army was able to charge across and completely destroy the city of Tyre.

And even today, as a result of what Alexander did, there is a peninsula now that goes out there instead of just an island. The prophecy had said, "I will scrape away her rubble and make her a bare rock, and you'll be able to spread fishnets where the city used to be." You might think that's figurative language. In this case, it was extremely literal — and Alexander was fulfilling that prophecy, even though he probably had no idea it had been written.

### Daniel 2: The Bronze Kingdom

In the book of Daniel, there are several prophecies about Alexander. In chapter 2, you have the dream of Nebuchadnezzar — that great statue representing the empires that would rule over the world. Nebuchadnezzar was the head of gold. What came after? The shoulders and arms of silver — that's Medo-Persia. Then the chest and loins of bronze. Then the legs of iron and the feet of iron mixed with clay. When you get to the bronze section of that statue, that represented the kingdom of the Greeks — the third kingdom that would rule over the whole earth.

### Daniel 7: The Leopard with Four Wings

In Daniel 7, there's a similar prophecy where Daniel sees various beasts representing those same empires. The one that represented the Greeks was a leopard. It says the leopard had four wings like that of a bird, and this beast had four heads. A leopard, as you know, is a predator — a fast runner, able to leap out of a tree. If it has wings, it can go even faster. That perhaps refers to the fact that Alexander was such a rapid conqueror, moving so quickly through thousands of miles. And the four heads are a reference to the four divisions of the Greek empire when Alexander died.

### Daniel 8: The Goat and the Ram

In Daniel 8, we have two animals: a ram and a goat. The ram represented the Persian Empire, and the goat was the Greek Empire. The goat had a prominent horn between his eyes — that represented its first king. The goat came and charged at the ram, attacking furiously, striking the ram, shattering its two horns of Media and Persia, knocking it to the ground and trampling on it. It goes on to say the goat became very great, and at the height of his power his large horn was broken off — Alexander at the height of his power, suddenly gone. And four horns come out in its place, representing the four divisions of his kingdom — four generals who served under him, dividing his kingdom after his death.

### Daniel 10–11: Angelic Warfare and the Prince of Greece

Finally, in Daniel 10 and 11, near the end of Daniel's life, he is having visions from God. An angel comes to him and says, "I have to return to fight against the Prince of Persia, and when I go, the Prince of Greece will come." This is a very interesting statement. The angel mentions Michael, an angel who fights for the people of God — but angels fight on a different level than we do. The Bible presents the idolatrous kingdoms of the world as actually serving demons or evil angels under Satan. When we look at history we see humans fighting each other, but Daniel here is given a glimpse of what's going on above the humans — sort of like the story of Elijah, with angels circling around the city that nobody could see, but they were there.

The decisions made by human rulers are decisions where they're influenced either for good or for evil by God or by Satan. These empires are idolatrous, godless, and they follow the leading given to them by Satan. But God is sovereign over all events, including over Satan himself. When one of Satan's plans agrees with God's purpose, God allows Satan to carry that out; if it goes against what God has planned, God frustrates that plan. The prince of Greece was not Alexander, but some angelic evil power that was leading Alexander. And God's angels were fighting on our behalf in these cases.

Daniel 11 also says there will be a series of kings in Persia, and then someone will stir up everyone against the kingdom of Greece — Xerxes did this. Then "a mighty king will appear who will rule with great power and do as he pleases" — that's Alexander. And when he has finished, his empire will be broken up and divided to the four winds of heaven.

All these prophecies were written 200 years before Alexander's life.

## II. How God Gave Alexander His Empire

The Greeks were not a nation — they were several little city-states, loosely connected and often fighting each other. When the Persians attacked from the east, they banded together to defend themselves. That involved the famous Spartan soldiers — 300 of them. Some of you may have caps that say "Come and take it" — that's what the Spartans said to the Persians when told to give up their weapons: come and get them. The Spartans hardly ever got along with Athens, but for this particular war they did, because they were in it together. The Athenians had a tremendous navy; the Spartans were excellent on land. Combining their forces, they were able to hold back a huge attacking force — estimated around a million men in navy, army, and support — from a relatively small population. They did it. Xerxes was defeated and had to retreat back to Turkey.

Greece was divided into various districts, and the northern part was the kingdom of Macedonia. The ruler of Macedon was Philip of Macedon, a great war leader who subdued the other parts of Greece under his authority. It was sort of a full-time job just keeping his influence throughout Greece. Then Philip was assassinated, and his young son Alexander became king in his place.

Alexander was trained as a Greek scholar. He loved Greek literature and philosophy. His private tutor was Aristotle — the great intellect. Alexander was only 20 when the authority of government was thrust upon him. After the murder of his father, he became king at the age of 20. There are conspiracy theories that Alexander himself arranged it — there's quite a bit of controversy about that.

Alexander first wanted to consolidate the Greek city-states behind himself. Down a bit to the south of Macedon is the city of Thebes, and that city revolted against Alexander. He went to Thebes and utterly destroyed that city — he killed many of the people and sold the rest into slavery. This had never been done to fellow Greeks, but he did it, and it put fear and dread of him throughout the Greek-speaking world. After that, no one dared to rebel against him in Greece itself.

He then took a small army of Macedonians to attack the Persians in modern-day Turkey. He also took scientists, historians, and others with him — reminds me of Napoleon, who did the same thing when he went to Egypt. They crossed the Dardanelles, that small body of water between Greece and Turkey, and came to the city of Troy — the same city you read about in the Iliad — right up in the corner of Turkey. That's the same area where the Apostle Paul was when he saw the vision of the man from Macedonia, and Paul then went to Macedonia from Troy to bring the gospel. But 300 years earlier, Alexander came from Macedonia to Troy to conquer it. One came conquering militarily, the other came conquering with the gospel. Which had the most lasting significance? I think we know the answer.

Here's a brief survey of Alexander's conquering campaign — it took about 10 years with his small army. As soon as he crossed over to Troy and started marching into the mainland, the Persians came up to confront him. The Emperor of Persia was Darius the Third, but Darius himself was not present at this first battle. His large army was under his various generals, and it seemed like it would be an easy victory — they vastly outnumbered the Greeks. But surprisingly, the Greeks defeated them.

Alexander then proceeded to march down the coast of Turkey, across the southern part, making a loop through the middle, and got to the east side of Turkey where it meets the corner of the Mediterranean Sea — the place of Issus, where there was a second great battle. Darius was present at that battle, and again the Greeks defeated the much larger Persian army. Darius ran away, and Alexander continued his march south down the coast of Palestine. He encountered Tyre — as we discussed. He went on down into Egypt, where the Egyptians were so scared of him that they surrendered very quickly and made him Pharaoh. One of his generals, after Alexander died, declared himself Pharaoh, and the last famous person in that line was Cleopatra.

From Egypt he came back, headed toward Gaza and then Jerusalem — we'll come back to that. He then moved east to the area near Assyria, where he fought his third great battle against the Persians at Gaugamela. Darius tried to run away, but his own servants killed him later and brought his head to Alexander. After defeating the Persians there, Alexander was the great power in the whole Persian Empire.

He continued marching, taking various cities, and went all the way east through Parthia to Bactria, where he became more and more like an eastern despot. He adopted the clothing of eastern monarchs, no longer the Greek leaning toward democracy. He made all his soldiers bow down before him. He married Roxana, a princess of Bactria. They had a son together. He went all the way down to the Indus River — and then the soldiers rebelled. That's enough. They wanted to go home. He wanted to keep going forever, but they forced him to return west.

He led them down south, then traveled west along a terrible desert route that killed a high percentage of his soldiers by thirst and disease. When they reached Babylon, Alexander himself fell ill and died young, from disease and dissipation.

### The Phalanx

Alexander's military success was based on a way of fighting totally new and different from anything the Persians had seen. The phalanx formation was a tightly packed block of men with long pikes, shields, and swords, marching together like a block. If you attacked them with horses or chariots, they would lower their long pikes, and the horses would skewer themselves. The horses were afraid of that and veered off. As they rode past, the soldiers were able to kill them. This decimated the Persian cavalry forces. The Persian infantry were not nearly as disciplined, were more spread out, and were easy picking for these phalanx blocks of soldiers.

### Alexander and Jerusalem

When Alexander went through the city of Jerusalem, he didn't fight there. There is a story the Jews tell, recorded in Josephus. We don't know for sure if it's true, but it's a great story. The high priest had a dream that Alexander was coming and that he had fulfilled these prophecies in Ezekiel and Daniel. Alexander was going to make terrible demands upon Jerusalem because they hadn't helped him on his way south. But the story is that the high priest went out of Jerusalem to meet Alexander and showed him the scrolls of Scripture where he was predicted. Alexander was so pleased that he gave the city many blessings instead of harsh terms.

## III. How Alexander Prepared the Way for the Greek New Testament

After Alexander died, his infant son was murdered immediately because his generals did not want any competition. They divided up his kingdom. The two families most significant in Scripture were the Ptolemies, who ruled Egypt, and the Seleucids, who ruled in Syria. Both of those families ruled over the Jews at different times, and they fought over the Jewish territory. Sometimes from the north the Syrians would come down; sometimes from the south, the Egyptians would come up. The Jews were constantly being attacked one way or the other through the next 200 years after Alexander died.

But here is Alexander's lasting significance. Aristotle, his teacher, gave him a love of Greek culture and learning. Alexander loved Greek literature, and everywhere he went, when his generals took over they continued his policy of using Greek as the official language of government and high culture. So throughout all the region he conquered, including the Middle East, Greek became the common language.

The Greek that was spoken was called Hellenistic Greek — the Greek spoken throughout the empire, as opposed to Hellenic Greek, the Greek spoken by Greeks living in Greece itself. Sort of like American English versus British English. When the New Testament was written in Greek, it could be understood and read throughout all the areas these rulers controlled. We read in the book of Acts that as Paul went from place to place, there were different local languages — but they all could speak Greek. There is a Greek manuscript from that period preserved 2,000 years old using that type of Hellenistic Greek, and the Greek New Testament that we have and rely upon as the Word of God is written in that same language.

It's wonderful to see how even today we can study the very words that God inspired.

God is sovereign, and he used Alexander just as he uses other world leaders — to accomplish his purposes, spread his Word, build up his church, and make lasting changes in the world that these great rulers have no idea about. The most humble, most unknown Christian is more precious in God's sight than the greatest conqueror — and yet God uses even the conquerors to build his church.
